293.73%

Source: Damodaran, Seeking Alpha, and author calculations

Strong Buy

In light of industry developments, the initial thesis supporting TOYO as a value-driven buy persists. The company has demonstrated strong execution, deftly navigating a dynamic geopolitical terrain while investing strategically in new markets. With 2026 capacity targets on track and the Houston facility ramping production, TOYO appears well-positioned to capitalize on U.S. demand for domestically manufactured modules. The combination of geographic diversification, expanding production capacity, and improving margins as facilities mature supports continued outperformance potential.

data.title: DFIV: International Value With Acceptable Risks, But Peers Are More Attractive
data.description:
Dimensional International Value ETF

The Dimensional International Value ETF (DFIV) has been a very stable performer in the past decade, most recently supported by its circa 47% rally in the past twelve months.

DFIV is an excellent global diversifier because it focuses heavily on Japanese and European equities, while avoiding the loftier valuations found in US markets. Many of these markets, such as the EU, trade at a strong historical discount to the United States and offer slightly higher dividend yields.

Even after this stellar rally, major international large-cap companies still trade at more compelling valuations and offer higher dividend yields. Both Japanese and European equities appear equally reasonably valued, and a globally diversified products like these look like a safe hold in 2026.

One significant risk with this ETF is that Japan and the EU's economies will have slower growth in 2026. Its top holdings also include commonly held large-cap stocks, which may be more vulnerable to pullbacks in 2026. Alternative value funds and other funds that focus more on smaller companies in 2026 may have an edge in this environment.

Overall, diversifying away from the United States by adding exposure to Japan and the EU looks like a solid strategy for 2026, and DFIV offers this appeal. However, the valuation of the MSCI World Ex. US, this ETF's benchmark, has risen, and these equities are only fairly priced at the moment. There are several more attractively priced and higher-yielding peers to consider, which can collectively provide geographical diversification benefits to one's portfolio. DFIV is an interesting vehicle, but it doesn't fully capture the value in these global equities.

Dimensional International Value ETF Overview

The Dimension International Value ETF (DFIV) holds 489 leading international value stocks, primarily in developed markets (except for the USA). DFIV has a 0.27 expense ratio and manages over $16.5 billion in assets.

One of the main appeals and differentiators of DFIV is that it tracks the MSCI World Ex. US Index, which helps investors avoid the loftier valuation and lower yield of the US market. This index came into the spotlight in 2025 after decades of relative underperformance.

DFIV is quite diversified in terms of company holdings, primarily targeting major large-cap stocks in various international markets. Unlike many other ETFs, it does not make concentrated bets on single companies, as no company accounts for more than 3% of its total assets. Its top 10 holdings include dominant large-cap companies in markets like Japan and Europe.

DFIV’s portfolio is roughly in line with its benchmark index, although it has some differences, such as its average market cap, industry weightings (underweight finance and overweight energy), and its country allocation (slightly overweight Japan).

One of the reasons why this ETF's index trades at a strong discount to the MSCI World Index is because of its industry approach, as it invests in industries like financials, energy, and materials, and only has minimal exposure to consumer stocks. Moreover, it does not have significant exposure to IT stocks, which have been one of the main drivers of the S&P 500 and MSCI World Index.

However, DFIV has still held its ground during the past five years, when US growth themes dominated the markets, and has even slightly outperformed these ETFs during the past five years.

Country Economic Outlooks

The second area to monitor when considering this ETF is the economic health of Japan and the European Union, as these are some of the main areas that this ETF targets.

Japan faces a challenging 2026, with weaker growth and a potential mild hike in interest rates. Export growth will also likely continue to stagnate due to lower exports to the United States and other key export markets.

Despite these headwinds, Japan's stock market has still been resilient, and corporate earnings growth has been healthy. Companies have been successful in their export markets and have also been able to successfully implement price hikes.

Japanese equities had one of their best years in over a decade last year, and will likely be more stagnant in 2026.

European equities look like an intriguing area to monitor, as valuations remain compelling following their sluggish performance in 2025. JP Morgan projects that European stocks will rally by 14.8% this year, slightly outperforming the S&P 500.

European equities are currently very attractive from a relative valuation perspective, trading at a significant historical discount to US equities. A 20% discount would be more in line with its historical average.

This displays how valuations have run ahead of themselves in the US markets, while valuations in the EU remain reasonably priced. EU equities currently trade in line with Japanese equities at the moment, so while they are reasonably priced, they are not an absolute steal at the moment.

DFIV: Risks and Alternatives

The Dimensional International Value ETF has been a historical outperformer in recent years, particularly delivering large capital gains in 2025. Some of the key sectors it targets, such as global financials, have performed very well in recent years, but may face slight risks as growth moderates in regions like the EU in 2026. DFIV is also very heavily exposed to energy and materials stocks, with limited consumer exposure, which likely makes room for multiple contractions.

Another mild concern with DFIV is that its yield has declined slightly following its 2025 run, which may make other international value funds stand out in the market. At the moment, its dividend yield is slightly under 3%, which makes it ideal to consider other international peer ETFs with slightly higher yields.

International value funds rallied in 2025, with DFIV ahead of the pack, which has resulted in slightly lower yields in many of these products for investors who choose to enter in 2026.

MSCI Value

ETFs like the iShares MSCI EAFE Value ETF (EFV) may be poised to outperform due to the higher yield and favorable valuation (15x earnings). This ETF has a similar country approach as DFIV, as it primarily focuses on Japanese and European equities and has a heavy weighting in financial stocks.

This ETF trades at a slight PER and PBR discount to the MSCI Europe Index, making it an intriguing value play in the market to consider, and also currently yields 4.1%. This ETF also has lower exposure to energy and materials stocks.

Japan Value Investing

Japanese equities are in a similar situation, as multiples have expanded strongly after its 12 month rally. Adding exposure to small caps is another way to access some of these markets at a discount. The WisdomTree Japan SmallCap ETF (DFJ) is a unique vehicle that allows investors to access Japan's stock market at a significant discount to the MSCI Japan Index.

At the moment, this ETF trades at a circa 23% discount to the MSCI World Ex. US Index and the MSCI Japan Index. This ETF also provides considerable exposure (27%) to Japanese mid-cap stocks.

Both DFJ and EFV are solid alternatives to DFIV, and would collectively offer similar exposure to reasonably priced stocks in Japan and the EU, providing some of the similar geographical diversification objectives of the MSCI World EX-US Index.

At the moment, it appears best to hold DFIV and wait for stronger pullbacks during H1 2026, and to consider other alternatives that have a greater margin of safety due to the lower valuations/higher yields.

data.title: Kraft Heinz: Institutional Investors Likely Reaching A Tipping Point
data.description:
Sometimes that status quo becomes unacceptable. While there are times that investors will tacitly accept having a bad or underperforming investment in a portfolio because that financial option isn't significantly undermining the overall investing performance of an account, at some point, most people reach a breaking point with these kinds of decisions.

The Kraft Heinz Company (KHC) has been one of the most disappointing companies in the market for nearly a decade now. This retail consumer consistently and significantly underperformed both most peers and the S&P 500 (SPY).

The Kraft Heinz Company has offered investors total returns of 47.80 percent over the last decade, while the S&P 500 has offered investors total returns of 334.2 percent during the same time period.

I last wrote about KHC in August of 2025, and I rated the company a strong sell, primarily because of the company's inability to grow mature brands, on which management relies for earnings and revenues. Today, I am reiterating my strong sell rating. I wanted to revisit my coverage of KHC since the company recently reported earnings and announced a plan to split the food seller's consumer businesses into two companies. Kraft-Heinz has also been relying on what is increasingly clear to be unsustainable price increases, and with most middle- and low-income consumers facing tough choices and a weaker economy, the company can no longer rely on pricing power.

The Kraft Heinz Company's last earnings should raise multiple red flags for investors. Management recently stated that third-quarter earnings per share normalized actual came in at $.61 a share, and revenue actual was $6.24 billion. While the company slightly beat very muted EPS expectations of $.58 a share, management also missed revenue forecasts by $22 million. The company also reported net sales fell by 2.3 percent on a year-to-year basis, and organic net sales fell by 2.5 percent annually. Management also stated that the company's adjusted gross profit margins fell by 200 basis points. While this well-known company was able to achieve some short-term earnings growth by cutting costs, KHC's guidance also shows that the company does not have a clear plan to address the multiple significant issues the leadership team of its food seller is struggling with. The Kraft Heinz Company reiterated that plans to split the company into two companies, Global Taste Elevation and North American Grocery Co., should be completed by the second half of 2026.

KHC gets nearly 75 percent of the company's revenues from more mature markets such as North America, where core brands The Kraft Heinz Company relies heavily on increasingly appear to have reached a point of saturation.

The market share of Heinz is nearly 80 percent in Europe and 60 percent in the US. KHC's Kraft Mac and Cheese brand has nearly 39 percent of this market in the US, down from nearly 45 percent in 2022. Finally, The Kraft Heinz Company's Philadelphia cream cheese brand has nearly two-thirds of this market in the US. KHC's core problem is that the company's brands are not growing in the most important markets in which they operate, and management's pricing power isn't driving earnings or revenue growth anymore either. The decision to break the company into 2 separate entities does not address any of the core issues that KHC has faced for years, which is why Warren Buffett has stated his clear opposition to this decision by KHC's leaders.

The Kraft Heinz Company's revenues and earnings have stagnated for some time; management should be focused on trying to drive more growth outside of North America in addition to developing new brands.

KHC's revenues have actually declined by nearly $1.7 billion since 2016, and the company's earnings growth has slowed significantly as well.

The Kraft Heinz Company has also accumulated a significant amount of debt, and management has recently stated plans to use existing cash flow to reduce these liabilities instead of investing in the company's struggling core brands.

While KHC's current long-term debt level of nearly $19 billion is down from the nearly $31 billion in long-term debt The Kraft Heinz Company was dealing with in 2020 during the pandemic, the company's current market cap is nearly $27 billion.

This is why The Kraft Heinz Company looks overvalued at 8x expected forward EBITDA. Even though KHC's 5-year average valuation is 10.19 forecasted forward EBITDA, the well-known company is seeing negative sales and revenue numbers, as well as anemic earnings growth. Analysts are expecting the company to grow earnings at just 4-6 percent over the next 5 years, and these projections look too optimistic, with 19 of the last earnings revisions being downward, and KHC facing significant challenges growing core brands.

The company is also completing a $3 billion share buyback program, and management will likely be constrained from authorizing new and significant share buybacks because of the company's significant debt levels.

All investment theses have risks, and KHC has brought debt levels down notably without having to reduce dividend payments since 2019. The company's market share and revenues also remain stable, and if forex moves become more favorable, the company will likely see some slight tailwinds from the business operations in Europe and emerging markets. Management may be able to focus more on the core brands after the company downsizes in the second half of 2026, which could be a positive as well, although most of these labels appear to have clearly reached a point of saturation.

When The Kraft Heinz Company reports fourth-quarter earnings in early February, I expect the company's net sales growth to continue to be negative, within the low single-digit range, and I do not believe management will detail a specific plan to reinvest in the company's core brands. The key metric to evaluate will be organic net sales growth, particularly outside of the US. KHC's long-term debt levels, which remain high despite coming down notably, are important to take note of as well. Finally, management has not yet clearly articulated what, if any, significant plans the company has to reinvest or help grow the more mature core products that have struggled to take market share in the main markets KHC operates in.

There are many times when strong brands don't equate to good investments, and The Kraft Heinz Company has not been able to drive any consistent or substantive earnings growth for some time. While the well-known company's core brands are mostly maintaining existing market share levels, management has also not articulated a clear and comprehensive plan to grow market share or expand into new markets either. While The Kraft Heinz Company's dividend should remain stable, investors will likely be able to find better value elsewhere.
